It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: - SPACE toggles the white dot between the two cross centers (blue cross at ~45,36 and yellow cross at ~27,21) - When leaving a cross, its segments merge into one object - White dot is now back at blue cross center (45, 36) - Need to test movement (UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT) to see if white dot moves
- Goal model: Unknown - need to probe. The white dot may be a controllable cursor. The crosses and colored boxes may be targets or collectibles.
- Plan: Probe with SPACE first to see if it triggers any interaction, then try movement.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.

When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Now I see the pattern:
- The blue cross moved! It was centered at (45, 36) and is now centered at (42, 39).
- The white dot is at (42, 39) - the center of the blue cross.
- The blue cross follows the white dot when moving with arrow keys.
- The 'w' node at (63, 61-63) is just part of the HUD bar.
So the white dot is attached to the blue cross. When I move with arrow keys, the entire blue cross moves. SPACE teleports the white dot to the other cross center.
The 61 cells changed makes sense: the blue cross is 52 cells (4×13), plus the old position cleared and new position drawn, plus the white dot.
So the mechanics are:
1. Arrow keys move the blue cross (with white dot at center)
2. SPACE teleports to the other cross center
Goal hypothesis: I need to move the blue cross to collect or interact with the colored dots in the gray boxes. The blue cross should collect blue dots, and maybe I need to switch to the yellow cross to collect yellow dots.
Let me try moving the blue cross to a blue dot position to see what happens. The nearest blue dot is at (24, 40). The cross center is at (42, 39). I need to move UP to reach row 24.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model updated:
- White dot is attached to the blue cross center. Arrow keys move the entire blue cross.
- Blue cross center now at (42, 39), moved from (45, 36) → (42, 36) → (42, 39)
- SPACE teleports between cross centers
- Blue cross moves 3 cells per arrow key press
- Goal hypothesis: Move crosses to collect colored dots in gray boxes (blue cross collects blue dots, yellow cross collects yellow dots)

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current blue cross center at (42, 39)
# Blue dots at: (16,48), (24,40), (24,53), (35,48)
# Let me try moving the blue cross to a blue dot position
# Nearest: (24, 40) - need UP 6 cells (2 presses) and RIGHT 3 cells (1 press)
# But let me first try just one UP to confirm movement

action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
